story:
I run my hand along the scalloped edge of the table we shared for years, remembering how I set it for the two of us—sometimes fancy and romantic, sometimes just sandwiches and chips. It feels strange to set it now for someone else.

I lay the silverware first, trying to remember the proper place settings your family cared so much about—especially your mother. You came from a world of rules and ease; I came from a world that didn’t have oyster forks. I still hear her mocking my manners the first time your parents visited. I felt so small until I heard you say you planned to marry me because I mattered more to you than any oyster fork. After that, it became our joke, and we collected oyster forks we never used, just to laugh.

I didn’t think I could love you more fiercely than I did then, when you were strong and vibrant. I wonder now what loving someone else will feel like. I wonder if I’m truly ready.

I set out the good plates—the ones with little violets around the edge you gave me for our fourth anniversary. I trace the pattern and remember our first date, when you realized too late you had no flowers and picked violets from my neighbor’s garden. That day you learned they were my favorite, and you decided they were yours, too. We were so young. I wonder what his favorite flower will be.

I smooth the lace tablecloth we bought for our thirteenth anniversary, now a soft antique cream. You once spilled red sauce on your side. We tried everything, but the stain stayed, a tiny mark only we noticed. I always set that side for you and smiled when you laughed at my care. I tap the small stain beside the plate and wonder what lasting changes he will make.

I place the heavy silver candlesticks we bought for our twenty-fifth. You teased me that they were ugly and looked like something from Clue, and I nicknamed you Colonel Mustard. You still wanted me whenever I brought them out. I wonder if they can kindle similar feelings tonight.

I pour wine into the crystal glasses with tiny rubies in the stems, a gift for our fortieth anniversary. Guilt pinches as I use them for someone else, but you wouldn’t want them to gather dust.

From the kitchen comes the rich scent of chicken cacciatore. It was your favorite. I brought it to the hospital once like contraband. You were so tired of Jell-O and so tired in general, and when you laughed at the first bite, I cried—your laugh had been gone so long. The nurse tried to take it away, but you told her my cooking was essential to your recovery. You didn’t recover, but the food gave you joy. I wonder if he’ll like this meal, too.

I sit and let the weight of us press on my chest—our dinners, our promises, our forever. You made me swear not to close myself away. I almost broke that vow, content to live in our shadow. Then I met him. He swept me off my feet despite my cement shoes and woke my sleeping heart. This table is rich with your ghost, but you don’t haunt me like I feared.

The doorbell rings. On the sideboard, our wedding photo glows—our faces shining just after the preacher told us to kiss. I touch the carved frame, then move it from its place of honor to a spot behind the photo of our son at his college graduation.

You wanted me to move on when you were gone. And now it’s time.
